chunk_summary: This section discusses tele-counselling workflows for infant feeding, focusing on portion estimation using Indian household measures and photo-assisted recalls to improve dietary tracking and counseling outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the two-step method for portion estimation using household measures and photo calibration.
Learn strategies to handle mixed dishes and hidden energy sources in Indian diets during tele-nutrition counseling.
Explore culturally acceptable energy-dense additions for addressing undernutrition in infants and young children.
key_facts: Portion estimation uses household measures like katori, tablespoon, ladle, roti diameter, and handful, recorded as counts per day rather than calories.
Photo calibration involves capturing a typical meal photo next to a standard object (e.g., spoon or matchbox) to build a family-specific portion dictionary.
Mixed dishes are assessed by identifying the main cereal base, protein source, and visible oil/fat added.
High-yield hidden energy sources in Indian diets include oil/ghee, sweetened beverages, packaged snacks, and health drinks.
Energy-dense additions for undernutrition include oil/ghee, nut paste (age-appropriate), milk, curd, egg, and legumes, with affordability verification.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Complementary feeding of infants and young children aged 6–23 months should focus on culturally appropriate energy-dense additions and portion estimation methods. (Dietary Guidelines for Indians, ICMR-NIN, WHO, 2023)
